title: Chapter 13: Compliance
content: The pill is small and blue and bitter on my tongue before I even swallow.

That's the thing they don't tell you about antipsychotics — the coating dissolves the second it hits saliva, and then you're tasting chemicals, something acrid and medicinal that coats the back of your throat like a promise. Or a threat. I lift the water glass with my right hand. I place the pill on my tongue with my left. I drink. I smile.
"Thank you, Mrs. Grose. The orange juice is lovely today."

It is lovely. Fresh-squeezed, not from concentrate, the kind of petty luxury that makes this house feel like a gift instead of a cage. The warmth of it spreads through my chest. I hold the warmth there, let it register on my face — the pleasant docility of a woman who has finally accepted her diagnosis.

Miles watches from across the table. His fork hovers over his eggs, suspended mid-bite, and his eyes track the movement of my throat as I swallow. He looks satisfied. His shoulders drop a fraction of an inch. The line between his brows — the one that's been living there since Dr. Richards's visit — smooths out.
Flora watches too. But Flora's expression is harder to read. She's not looking at my throat. She's looking at my eyes.

I make sure to blink slowly. Lazily. The way a medicated person blinks — like the world is coming to them through gauze, like alertness is a country they've vacated. I reach for my juice again. I take a long, peaceful sip.

What they don't see: the pill tucked against my left cheek, lodged in the pocket between my molars and my gums. The bitterness blooming there, intimate and controlled. I'll spit it into a tissue later, the way I've done every morning for six days. I've gotten good at it — the discreet turn of the head, the casual dab at the corner of my mouth, the tissue dropped into my pocket and later transferred to the zipper compartment of my suitcase. I have nineteen pills lined up in a clean sock now, like little blue soldiers waiting for orders.
But not every day. Some days I swallow.

That's the part that matters. Some days — every third or fourth day, rotated irregularly so my body can't predict it — I take the pill for real. I let the chemicals enter my bloodstream. I let the fog roll in, just a little, just enough to build tolerance, to make the story airtight. Because the thing about faking medication is that you have to account for the side effects you'd have if you were actually taking it. Miss a beat — show up too sharp, too clear-eyed on a day when you should be swimming through molasses — and the whole performance collapses.
So I swallow. I let my words slur slightly. I let my reactions lag by a half-second. I let the world soften around the edges, and then I wait for it to pass, and then I get back to work.
This is what compliance looks like from the inside.
"You're right, Miles. I must have imagined it."

I say this on Day 17. We're in the east parlor, the one with the gray light that makes everyone look unwell. Miles has just finished explaining — gently, patiently, the way you'd explain to a child or a convalescent — that the sounds I heard in the west wing were probably pipes. Old house. Old pipes. Completely normal.
"Old houses make all kinds of noises," he says. His voice is warm. Reassuring. It's the voice of someone who has won. "You'll feel better now that the medication's kicking in."
"I already do," I say. I fold my hands in my lap. I look at them — my hands, pale and still — and then I look up at him with what I hope is gratitude. "Thank you. For being patient with me."

Something flickers in his face. A micro-expression, there and gone. I think it might be doubt, or maybe just the unfamiliar sensation of being thanked by someone you've been actively gaslighting for two weeks. But it passes. He nods. He leaves.
Flora lingers.
"Did you sleep better?" she asks. Her voice is light, conversational, but her eyes are doing that thing again — searching, careful, like she's reading a document in a language she almost speaks.
"Much better," I say. "Thank you, Flora."

She tilts her head. Just a degree. A bird examining something that might be a threat or might be a seed. Then she smiles, and it's a perfect smile, and she floats out of the room on her particular current of spectral grace, and I sit very still and feel the skin on the back of my neck prickle.
Flora is the one to watch.

Miles is the architect — he builds the trap, sets the cameras, orchestrates the gaslighting. But Miles is also arrogant, and arrogance makes you sloppy. Flora is something else. Flora pays attention. Flora notices patterns. Flora is the one who left the tea outside my door every night — not Miles — and she stopped doing it on Day 19, not because she believes I'm better, but because she's testing whether I'll notice the absence.
I noticed.
I didn't say anything. That's the game now.
By Day 20, the rhythm of the house has shifted, and I have cataloged every beat.

Miles goes to the control room at 11 PM. I know this because I pressed my ear to the corridor wall and listened for the distant hum of the monitors powering up, the soft beep of the security system cycling through camera feeds. He stays until 1 or 2 AM. Sometimes later if he's editing footage — I've learned to distinguish the click of a mouse from the click of a keyboard, and the keyboard means he's writing something. Captions, probably. Metadata. The architecture of a narrative he's building about the tutor who went mad in his house.

Flora sneaks to the garden at dawn. I don't know what she does there — I haven't been able to follow without risking the cameras — but I hear the back door at 5:47 AM every morning, and I hear it close again at 6:30. She moves lightly, like someone who doesn't want to be heard, which is interesting. What does Flora do when no one is watching? What does she tend in that garden? What does she bury?

Mrs. Grose locks the back kitchen at 9 PM. I hear the deadbolt turn — a solid, satisfying sound, metal fitting into metal, the click of a world being secured. She retires to her quarters on the ground floor after that, and the kitchen becomes a ghost town until 6 AM.
These are the intervals. The gaps. The spaces where things happen that no one is watching, and I file each one away, and I wait.

The daily gaslighting has eased to almost nothing. What's the point of tormenting someone who's already broken? Miles has stopped asking me leading questions about what I've seen. Flora has stopped appearing in doorways at inconvenient moments, her face arranged in that look of mild concern that used to make me question my own senses. They've stopped manufacturing evidence and started ignoring me instead, which is exactly what I needed them to do.

On Day 21, Miles leaves his phone on the kitchen counter for four full minutes while he steps outside to take a call. I don't touch it. I don't even look at it directly. But I note its position — counter, left side, near the toaster — and I note that he didn't lock it, and I note that when he comes back, he doesn't check whether it's been moved.

On Day 22, Flora leaves her laptop open on the library table while she goes to change her shoes. I'm in the next room, performing my afternoon sedation — sitting by the window with a book I'm not reading, my breathing slow and even. I hear her footsteps on the stairs. I wait thirty seconds. I walk to the library doorway and look.

The screen shows a folder. The folder is labeled BL.Y. Inside, I can see thumbnail icons — video files, dozens of them, with dates going back months before my arrival. I don't go closer. I don't touch anything. I turn around and go back to my window and pick up my book, and when Flora returns eleven minutes later, I'm the same blank-faced woman I've been since the pills started working.
But inside, the catalog expands. BL.Y. The project has a name. And it predates me.
The keys hang on a hook beside the pantry door.

I find them on Day 23, in the evening, while the twins are filming TikTok content in the library. I can hear them through the walls — Miles directing, Flora performing, the strange choreography of people who've turned their own lives into content. The key to the control room is on a red fob. The key to the attic is on a blue one. The key to the gate — the front gate, the one that locks this property against the world — is on a plain iron ring.

I stand in Mrs. Grose's kitchen and I look at them. The hook is brass, tarnished with age, mounted to the wall with two screws. The keys sway slightly, moved by the draft from the pantry door I left cracked.

I take out my phone. I photograph their position — the red fob on the left, the blue one in the center, the iron ring on the right. I photograph the hook and its mounting. I photograph the distance from the pantry door, which is roughly four paces. I photograph the deadbolt on the kitchen's back entrance, which I now know Mrs. Grose locks at 9 PM and which probably takes me twelve seconds to pick with a bobby pin — not that I've practiced, not that I've been sitting in my room with the lock from my suitcase, teaching my fingers the language of tumblers.

I don't take the keys. Not yet. Taking them would be a declaration. Taking them would be a move, and I'm not ready to make moves. I'm still in the phase where I gather. Where I watch. Where I let the web spin itself while I memorize every thread.

But I know which key opens which door. I know the path from my bedroom to this hook, and I know that at 11:07 PM on any given night, that path is unwatched. I know things now that I didn't know before I started taking the pills — or rather, before I started performing the act of taking the pills. And isn't that the cruelest joke of all? The medication was supposed to make me docile and foggy and manageable. Instead, it made me careful. It made me strategic. It made me the kind of person who can stand in a dark kitchen and photograph a set of keys and feel nothing but the clean, bright click of a plan falling into place.

I put my phone away. I leave the kitchen the way I came. I walk back to my room, slow and measured, the gait of a medicated woman, and I close the door and sit on my bed and I add the keys to the map I've been building in my journal, the one hidden beneath the lining of my suitcase.
The map is almost complete.
On Day 24, I take the satellite phone to the garden shed at 6 AM — safely between Flora's departure and Mrs. Grose's kitchen opening — and I type a Reddit post.

**u/BlitheSpirit_Maeve**
Taking a break from reddit. Feeling much better. The doctor was right — I just needed rest.

I stare at it for a long time. The words look wrong in my mouth. They taste like surrender, which is the point, but they also taste like something else — like a signal flare shot in the wrong direction, like a cry for help that I'm deliberately muffling.
I post it anyway.

The responses come within the hour, slow and glitchy over the satellite connection. @Detective_Z, who's been parsing my every word since Day 1: "This reads like someone posting under duress." @TrueCrimeJunkie: "Stay safe OP." Someone called @ScoopMcGee screenshots it and quotes the phrasing — 'feeling much better' is textbook coercion language, apparently. A small argument breaks out in the comments about whether I'm being held against my will or whether I'm just a drama queen who finally got the psychiatric help I needed.
I read every comment. I note the IP addresses visible in the thread metadata. I close the phone and bury it back under the loose floorboard in the shed.

The thread will cool. I know this. The internet has the attention span of a goldfish on amphetamines. By tomorrow, the algorithm will have moved on — some new horror, some new mystery, some new person in trouble who isn't me. The Reddit thread will drop off the front page. The true crime enthusiasts will decamp to fresher kills. And I will be alone in this house with the twins and the cameras and the blue pills I'm not taking, and no one on the outside will be watching.
That's the cost. That's the price of playing the long game — you have to let the cavalry ride away. You have to convince the world that you don't need rescuing, and then you have to rescue yourself.
But the thread doesn't die entirely. Two days later, when I check again, there's a new comment at the very bottom, posted from an account with no history and no avatar:
**u/throwaway_7644:** Read your first post again. You mentioned a name. Peter Quint. I knew him. He didn't leave Bly. He's still there.

I stare at the screen until it blurs. The shed is cold. The light through the cracks in the boards is gray and thin. I don't respond to the comment. I close the phone and I bury it and I walk back to the house with my fogged, medicated shuffle, and I think about Peter Quint, and I think about the folder labeled BL.Y, and I think about how much I don't know yet.
The map is almost complete. Almost.

I sit by the window in my room. The evening light is amber and long, the kind of light that makes Bly look almost beautiful — like a postcard, like a dream, like a place someone might choose to stay. My eyes are half-lidded, performing sedation. My hands rest in my lap, still and compliant. My breathing is slow, measured, the breathing of a woman whose mind has finally quieted.

Through the gap in my lashes, I watch Miles show Flora something on his phone. They're in the garden, visible through the lower half of my window, standing near the rose bushes that Flora tends at dawn. Miles laughs — a genuine laugh, head thrown back, careless. Flora smiles, but her attention is divided. She keeps glancing toward the house. Toward the windows.
Toward me.
I don't flinch. I don't move. I'm a shape behind glass, a silhouette, a woman rendered harmless by chemistry and compliance.

Miles shows her the phone again. They both laugh this time. They think I'm beaten. They think the pills are working. They think the tutor they broke is staying broken, and that's their mistake — the fundamental miscalculation that all abusers make, the assumption that submission equals defeat.

I count their steps from the rose bushes to the garden gate: fourteen for Miles, seventeen for Flora, who walks with that drifting, theatrical stride. I note that Miles keeps his phone in his left hand when he's relaxed, and in his right hand when he's alert. I note that Flora positions herself with her back to the house — she doesn't like the feeling of being watched, which means she knows what it feels like to be watched, which means she understands the cameras from the other side.

I file these observations alongside all the others. The schedules. The keys. The camera blind spots I've mapped by tracking the red recording lights that blink in the corners of every room. The gaps in the surveillance grid — the garden shed, the north corridor, the space behind the pantry door where the keys hang on their brass hook.

Under the compliance, something has been calcifying. Not into docility. Not into the soft surrender the pills were designed to produce. Something harder. Clearer. Something with facets that catch the light and throw it back sharper than it came.
I think the word is diamond.

It took geological pressure to make it — two weeks of lies and gaslighting and isolation and fear, two weeks of being told I was crazy until I almost believed it, two weeks of my reality being rewritten in real time by two children with cameras and cruelty and too much money. That pressure could have crushed me. It was supposed to crush me.
Instead, it compressed me into something new.

I shift slightly in my chair. The movement is small enough to read as restlessness, the kind of fidget a sedated person makes when they've been sitting too long. What it actually is: a recalibration. I'm adjusting my position so I can see the garden gate through the window's lower pane without moving my head.

Miles says something to Flora. She nods. They turn and walk back toward the house, their footsteps muffled by grass and distance. Miles has his right hand on his phone now. Alert. But not alert enough. Not vigilant the way he was on Day 1, or Day 5, or Day 12, when he watched me like I was a puzzle he hadn't solved yet. He's solved me now. He's put me in the "broken" column and moved on.

Flora glances up at my window one more time before they reach the door. I let my head loll slightly to the left, the way it does when the medication is pulling me under. I let my eyes drift nearly closed. I let her see what she expects to see: the tutor, fogged and docile, no threat to anyone.

Flora's gaze lingers for two seconds. Three. Then she looks away, and the back door opens, and they're inside, and I'm alone with the amber light and the window and the long, patient silence of a house that doesn't know it's being mapped.

I sit very still. I let the silence settle. I breathe in through my nose — the faint mustiness of old curtains, the copper tang of the radiator, the ghost of orange juice still lingering on my palate from this morning — and I breathe out through my mouth, slow and controlled, and I feel the diamond where it lives now, lodged behind my sternum like a second heart.
Then, so quietly that not even the cameras could catch it — a breath shaped into syllables, a sound that exists and then doesn't, a promise made to no one but myself — I whisper:
"Phase two."
